params.text:
What the law asks of your employer

No law bans lone working in adult social care. What the law requires is that the risks are assessed and controlled so far as is reasonably practicable — and that duty sits first with your employer. Section 2 of the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974 obliges them to protect your health, safety and welfare at work, and a lone visit is simply one more part of that.

  • Assess the risk (MHSWR 1999, reg 3): a suitable and sufficient assessment of working alone, reviewed when the person you support, the property or the round changes.
  • Tell you what it found (reg 10): you should be given clear information on the risks and the controls, not a file left in the office.
  • Prepare you properly (reg 13): your capability and training must be taken into account before you are asked to work alone.
  • Report certain incidents (RIDDOR 2013): specified injuries, over-seven-day absences and dangerous occurrences must go to the HSE.
  • Meet CQC standards: Regulation 12 requires safe care and treatment and Regulation 18 requires enough suitably skilled staff — a rota that leaves you unsupported can breach both.

HSE guidance INDG73 sets out what good practice looks like. Where a gross breach of a duty of care causes a death, and senior management failings are a substantial part of it, an organisation can be prosecuted under the Corporate Manslaughter and Corporate Homicide Act 2007.

params.alt: Two colleagues sharing responsibility for safety at work

params.text:
What you must do

The duties are not all one way. Section 7 of the 1974 Act places a legal duty on you personally, and section 8 makes it an offence to interfere with anything provided for safety.

  • Take reasonable care: for yourself and for anyone affected by what you do, including the person you support.
  • Co-operate with your employer: use the agreed system every time — check in, check out, carry the device you have been issued.
  • Do not misuse safety equipment: switching off an alarm or location tracker to save battery is exactly what section 8 prohibits.
  • Report what you meet: new hazards, near misses and aggression, so the assessment can be updated before someone is hurt.
  • Work within your competence: if you have not been trained for a task, say so before attempting it alone.

None of this makes you personally responsible for gaps your employer has left. Raising a concern is part of doing the job well.

params.question: A relative tells you that care workers 'are not allowed by law' to visit a property alone after dark. What is the correct legal position?

answers.text: There is no law banning lone working; the employer must assess the risks and control them so far as is reasonably practicable.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Lone working is lawful. The duty under the 1974 Act and the 1999 Regulations is to assess and control the risk, not to avoid lone working altogether.

answers.text: Lone working after dark is prohibited outright by the Health and Safety at Work etc. Act 1974.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Not quite. The Act creates general duties to protect health and safety; it sets no blanket ban on working alone at any time of day.

answers.text: It is lawful only if you sign a form accepting the risk yourself.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: No. An employer cannot transfer its section 2 duty to you by asking you to sign something. Your consent does not remove their responsibility.

answers.text: It is entirely your own decision, because section 7 puts the legal duty on the employee.

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Section 7 does give you a personal duty, but it sits alongside your employer's duty rather than replacing it. The risk assessment and safe system are still theirs to provide.
